sources.notes: Easy version asks for the lexicographically smallest permutation reachable by a strange triple operation.
Official/tester solutions greedily fix the next smallest value; the easy version can linearly scan feasible values each step.

annotation.summary: Find the lexicographically smallest permutation reachable by repeatedly applying the strange operation to triples of consecutive values.
annotation.constraints: The easy version permits O(n^2) scanning over remaining values.
annotation.core_idea: Process target values cnt=1..n. A value v can be reduced to cnt iff v has the same parity as cnt and all values in [cnt,v-1] are to its right, so the feasible choices are record-minimum positions when scanning values upward. Greedily place cnt at the leftmost feasible position; then conceptually reduce that value and increment all intervening values by 1, preserving a permutation of the remaining suffix.

solution_variants.name: quadratic-greedy-ranks
solution_variants.summary: For each next target value, scan remaining values to find the leftmost feasible same-parity record minimum.

solution_variants.name: local-operation-simulation
solution_variants.summary: Alternative O(n^2) implementation repeatedly applies the legal operation to local triples until no lexicographic decrease remains.

tags.evidence: The lexicographically smallest result is built by greedily fixing the smallest possible value at the earliest feasible position.
The feasibility condition depends on ranks and positions of consecutive values in the remaining permutation.
The easy version directly scans all remaining values at every step.
